23: A type: 'Thus says Cyrus king of Persia, The LORD, the God of heaven, has given me all the kingdoms of the earth, and he has charged me to build him a house at Jerusalem, which is in Judah. Whoever is among you of all his people, may the LORD his God be with him. Let him go up.' Cyrus's decree is Isaiah's prediction (Isaiah 44:28; 2 Chronicles 45:1-4 — naming Cyrus over a century before his birth) and Chronicles' fulfillment. Cyrus is called YHWH's 'anointed' (meshicho, Isaiah 45:1) — a Gentile king given the title used of the Davidic Messiah, showing that YHWH's sovereign purposes can work through unexpected agents. The pattern (a king's decree liberates an enslaved people to rebuild the temple) is the type for the NT's proclamation: the King of Kings' word liberates humanity from sin's exile to become the living temple of the Spirit (1 Corinthians 3:16-17).
1: A shadow. ‘The people of the land took Jehoahaz son of Josiah and made him king in his father’s place in Jerusalem’ — after Josiah’s death, the people bypass the normal succession and install their chosen son (bypassing the older Eliakim; cf. 2 Kings 23:31). Popular kingship-installation in the absence of a divine oracle is the kingdom’s final chapter without divine direction. The contrast with the Davidic line at its beginning: ‘the LORD said to Samuel, “I have chosen him”’ (1 Samuel 16:12). The people choosing their own king, rather than waiting for YHWH’s choice, is the shadow of every generation that prefers its own selection over the anointed one. Christ is the Father’s choice, announced at baptism: ‘this is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased’ (Matthew 3:17).
2: A shadow. Jehoahaz reigns three months — the briefest of brief reigns, insufficient for any substantive policy or reform. Three months in, then Egypt ends it. The rapidity of the collapse after Josiah’s death mirrors the pattern of the disciples after the crucifixion: scattered within hours (Mark 14:50), hiding behind locked doors (John 20:19). The three-month reign of Jehoahaz is less than the three days of the crucifixion. What the disciples could not sustain for three days, Jehoahaz could not sustain for three months. The short-lived successor who cannot maintain the inheritance is the type of all successors to Josiah until the one who does not merely succeed but fulfills.
3: A shadow. Egypt imposes a tribute of one hundred talents of silver and one talent of gold — the covenant people paying tribute to Egypt, the great type of bondage. The return to Egypt-tribute is the shadow of covenant slavery: the people who were redeemed from Egypt now pay Egypt again. Every tribute imposed by Gentile powers on Judah echoes the Egypt-bondage pattern that the Exodus was meant to terminate permanently. Christ pays the tribute that could not be owed — the price for sin that no coin could cover. But he also liberates from the tribute-paying condition: ‘you were ransomed from the futile ways inherited from your forefathers, not with perishable things such as silver or gold, but with the precious blood of Christ’ (1 Peter 1:18-19). The silver-and-gold tribute gives way to the blood-ransom.
4: A shadow. Egypt renames Eliakim as Jehoiakim — a pagan overlord renaming the Davidic king is the shadow of colonized identity, the covenant king re-branded by the empire. The name-change from ‘God establishes’ (Eliakim) to ‘YHWH establishes’ (Jehoiakim) is apparently a concession that preserves YHWH’s name but on Egypt’s terms. The colonized covenant identity is what Christ reverses: he gives his followers a new name (Revelation 2:17: ‘a new name that no one knows except the one who receives it’), a name not imposed by empire but given by the Father, the identity that no colonial power can rename.
5: A shadow. Jehoiakim’s eleven-year reign — the longest in the final four kings — is characterized entirely by ‘he did what was evil in the sight of the LORD his God.’ The king who reigns the longest of the final four is also the least faithful. The Chronicler does not detail the evil; the verdict is the whole record. The contrast with Christ: the one whose reign is ‘forever and ever’ (Revelation 11:15) does ‘what is right and just’ (Isaiah 9:7). The eleven years of Jehoiakim’s reign ‘doing evil’ is the anti-reign that Christ’s eternal righteous rule answers.
6: A shadow. Nebuchadnezzar binds Jehoiakim in bronze chains to take him to Babylon — the Davidic king in chains, led away by the Babylonian overlord. The binding of the king is the anti-anointing: what was promised to David (‘your house and your kingdom will endure forever,’ 2 Samuel 7:16) appears to be ending in chains and exile. The bound king is the type of the bound God: Christ in Gethsemane is bound and led away (John 18:12: ‘they bound him and led him first to Annas’). But the binding of the Son of God is the decisive un-binding of humanity from sin: ‘if the Son sets you free, you will be free indeed’ (John 8:36). The chains on the Davidic king give way to the chains on the divine king whose captivity is voluntary and redemptive.
7: A direct connection. ‘Nebuchadnezzar also carried some of the vessels of the LORD’s house to Babylon and placed them in his palace in Babylon’ — the sacred temple vessels transferred to the pagan palace treasury is one of the OT’s most theologically loaded acts. Daniel 5 makes these very vessels the focal point of Belshazzar’s sacrilege — when they are used for the pagan feast, the divine judgment-writing appears on the wall. The vessels represent the holiness of YHWH housed in Babylon. Christ himself is the vessel of divine holiness in a world of sacrilege: ‘in him the whole fullness of deity dwells bodily’ (Colossians 2:9). The sacred vessel in the hostile palace is the type of God present in a world that treats him as a trophy.
8: A shadow. ‘The rest of the acts of Jehoiakim and the abominations he committed, and what was found against him, are recorded in the Book of the Kings of Israel and Judah’ — the closing formula for Jehoiakim adds an unusual element: ‘what was found against him’ (wehannimṣāʾ ‘ālāyw). The phrase suggests a formal indictment; something was discovered that formed part of the legal case against him. This language of found evidence points toward the judgment scene: Revelation 20:12-13 describes books opened and people judged ‘according to what they had done.’ Jehoiakim’s ‘what was found against him’ is the precursor of the divine ledger; Christ is the advocate who stands before the same ledger on behalf of those he represents (1 John 2:1).
9: A shadow. Jehoiachin reigns three months and ten days — even briefer than Jehoahaz’ three months. The shrinking reign-lengths (11 years → 3 months → 3 months and 10 days → 11 years) tell the story of the kingdom’s accelerating collapse. The Davidic line, once measured in decades and centuries, is now measured in days. The trajectory is the darkness before dawn: the shortest reigns come just before the exile, but the exile ends with Cyrus’ decree — and ultimately with a king whose reign never ends (Luke 1:33: ‘of his kingdom there will be no end’). The three months and ten days of Jehoiachin’s reign is less than the time between Christ’s resurrection and ascension — yet in those days, a kingdom was established that still stands.
10: A shadow. ‘In the spring of the year, King Nebuchadnezzar sent and brought him to Babylon along with the precious vessels of the house of the LORD’ — Jehoiachin’s exile in ‘the spring of the year’ (lĕtěšûḇat haššānāh, ‘at the return of the year’, the spring military season) takes both the king and the remaining temple vessels. The spring is when kings go to war (2 Samuel 11:1) — but here it is when the Davidic king goes into exile. The Spring that should be victory becomes the spring of defeat. The reversal that Christ brings: the spring of his death and resurrection — the Passover season — reverses the spring of defeat into the spring of permanent victory. The season of exile becomes the season of new creation.
11: A shadow. Zedekiah — the last king, installed by Nebuchadnezzar himself — reigns eleven years. The Babylonian-installed king of Judah is the nadir of the Davidic dynasty: the king who rules by the permission of the exile power rather than by YHWH’s anointing. The king who rules by imperial permission is the type of all authority that is delegated rather than inherent. Christ’s authority is not delegated by empire: ‘all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me’ (Matthew 28:18) — given by the Father, not by Babylon. The emperor-permitted king and the Father-authorized king stand at opposite poles of the authority question.
12: A shadow. ‘He did what was evil in the sight of the LORD his God. He did not humble himself before Jeremiah the prophet, who spoke from the mouth of the LORD’ — Zedekiah’s specific failure is the failure to humble himself before the prophetic word. The verb niḵnaʿ (‘to humble oneself’) is the Chronicler’s key covenant term: Josiah humbled himself (2 Chronicles 34:27) and was blessed; Zedekiah refused and fell. The unwillingness to bow before the divine word is the core of human rebellion. Christ embodies the perfect humility: ‘though he was in the form of God... he humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death’ (Philippians 2:6-8). The self-humbling that Zedekiah refused, Christ enacted absolutely.
13: A shadow. ‘He also rebelled against King Nebuchadnezzar, who had made him swear by God. He stiffened his neck and hardened his heart against turning to the LORD, the God of Israel’ — the two rebellions: against the human overlord (breaking the sworn oath) and against YHWH (stiffened neck). The hiqšāh ‘ōrep (‘he stiffened the neck’) is the Exodus vocabulary of hardening (Exodus 32:9: ‘a stiff-necked people’). The last Davidic king repeats the sin of the wilderness generation. Stephen’s sermon names this same pattern: ‘you stiff-necked people... you always resist the Holy Spirit. As your fathers did, so do you’ (Acts 7:51). The stiff-necked king is the type of the generation that finally crucifies the Son.
14: A shadow. ‘Moreover, all the leading priests and the people greatly multiplied their unfaithfulness, following all the abominations of the nations, and they polluted the house of the LORD that he had made holy in Jerusalem’ — the collective corruption: priests, leaders, and people together polluting the sacred space. The pollution of the temple is the act that makes the exile inevitable (Jeremiah 7:12-15: ‘what I did to Shiloh’). Jesus’ temple cleansing (Matthew 21:12-13; John 2:14-17) is the reversal: rather than polluting, he purifies. His body is the new temple (John 2:21) that cannot be polluted by human unfaithfulness — ‘holy, innocent, unstained, separated from sinners’ (Hebrews 7:26). The priests who polluted the old temple give way to the High Priest who consecrates the new.
15: A direct connection. ‘The LORD, the God of their fathers, sent word to them persistently through his messengers because he had compassion on his people and on his dwelling place’ — the divine persistence in sending messengers is grounded in divine compassion (raḥam), not merely in covenant obligation. YHWH sends prophets not because he must but because he loves. Jesus quotes this exact pattern in the parable of the tenants (Matthew 21:33-46; Mark 12:1-12; Luke 20:9-19): ‘he sent his servants... he sent other servants... finally he sent his son.’ The persistent sending that 2 Chronicles 36:15 narrates is the structural backbone of the parable. The vineyard owner’s compassion that keeps sending messengers until the Son himself is sent is the same compassion the Chronicler attributes to YHWH here.
16: A direct connection. ‘But they kept mocking the messengers of God, despising his words and scoffing at his prophets, until the wrath of the LORD rose against his people, until there was no remedy’ — the triple rejection: mocking the messengers, despising the words, scoffing at the prophets. Jesus quotes this pattern explicitly: ‘Jerusalem, Jerusalem, you who kill the prophets and stone those who are sent to you’ (Matthew 23:37; Luke 13:34). The ‘no remedy’ (ʿad lĕʾên marpēʾ) is the diagnosis of a condition that has passed the point of prophylactic possibility — judgment is now inevitable. But what has ‘no remedy’ in the covenant of law finds remedy in the covenant of grace: the death that was irreversible for the covenant people becomes reversible through the resurrection. Christ is the ‘remedy’ where there was none.
17: A shadow. ‘Therefore he brought against them the king of the Chaldeans, who killed their young men with the sword in the house of their sanctuary and had no compassion on young man or virgin, old man or aged’ — the particular horror of the Babylonian assault: killing within the sanctuary, with no discrimination of age or gender. The sanctuary that was YHWH’s dwelling became the site of slaughter. The absence of compassion (lōʾ ḥāmal, ‘he did not spare’) mirrors the divine judgment language of Ezekiel: ‘my eye will not spare, and I will have no pity’ (Ezekiel 7:4). YHWH gave his people into the hand of one who would show them no mercy — but Christ stands as the one who, being given into the hand of the merciless, absorbed the judgment so that the people of God receive mercy instead of the Babylonian sword.
18: A shadow. The temple vessels carried to Babylon — ‘all the vessels of the house of God, great and small, and the treasures of the house of the LORD and the treasures of the king and his princes, all these he brought to Babylon’ — is the complete stripping of the sanctuary. Every sacred object, every royal treasure, taken by the empire. The despoiled temple is the type of the despoiled Christ: ‘they stripped him and put a scarlet robe on him’ (Matthew 27:28); the soldiers divided his garments (John 19:23-24, fulfilling Psalms 22:18). The stripping of the sanctuary and the stripping of the Son are both the removal of the sacred by the profane — but what Babylon carried to its palace, Christ carried to his resurrection. The vessels returned (Ezra 1:7-11); the garments were not needed again.
19: A shadow. ‘They burned the house of God and broke down the wall of Jerusalem. They burned all its palaces with fire and destroyed all its precious vessels’ — the complete destruction: the temple burned, the walls broken, the palaces consumed, the sacred objects destroyed. The burning of YHWH’s house is the nadir of the Davidic narrative — from Solomon’s glorious dedication (2 Chr 5-7) to ash. Jesus predicts the same event for the second temple: ‘there will not be left here one stone upon another that will not be thrown down’ (Matthew 24:2). The destruction of the temple is the sign of the end of the old covenant order — the burning of the Solomonic temple points to the destruction of the Herodian temple, which in turn points to the one who replaces the temple (John 4:21-24).
20: A shadow. ‘He took into exile in Babylon those who had escaped from the sword. They became servants to him and to his sons until the establishment of the kingdom of Persia’ — the covenant people as Babylonian servants ‘until the establishment of the kingdom of Persia.’ The time-limit is built into the exile: ‘until’ — not permanent servitude but bounded bondage. The ‘until’ is Daniel’s seventy years (Daniel 9:2, drawn from Jeremiah 25:11-12), and the political change of masters (Persia replacing Babylon) is YHWH’s instrument for the restoration. The bounded bondage points to Christ’s descent into death: not permanent death but bounded — ‘until the third day’ (Matthew 16:21; 2 Chronicles 17:23; 2 Chronicles 20:19). The resurrection is the ‘until’ point of death’s hold.
21: A direct connection. ‘This fulfilled the word of the LORD spoken through Jeremiah, until the land had enjoyed its Sabbaths. All the days of its desolation it rested, until seventy years were completed’ — the exile as Sabbath rest for the land is the Levitical theology of Leviticus 26:34-35 enacted: the land gets the Sabbath rests it was denied when Israel failed to observe the Jubilee and Sabbatical years. The land’s enforced rest is a shadow of the Sabbath rest that remains for the people of God (Hebrews 4:9-11: ‘there remains a Sabbath rest for the people of God’). The desolation that gives the land its rest points to the death that gives humanity its permanent rest — the exile’s enforced Sabbath is a dark foreshadowing of the rest that Christ’s death secures.
22: A direct connection. ‘Now in the first year of Cyrus king of Persia, in order to fulfill the word of the LORD spoken through Jeremiah, the LORD stirred up the spirit of Cyrus king of Persia to make a proclamation throughout all his kingdom and to put it in writing’ — the three convergences: the prophetic word of Jeremiah (seventy years), the anointing of Cyrus named by Isaiah (Isaiah 44:28; 2 Chronicles 45:1: ‘who says of Cyrus, “He is my shepherd”... thus says the LORD to his anointed, to Cyrus’), and YHWH’s stirring of the pagan king’s spirit. The first-year declaration of Cyrus is the type of Pentecost: ‘the spirit stirred up’ → proclamation throughout all the kingdom → written and spoken simultaneously. The Spirit that ‘stirred up’ Cyrus is the same Spirit poured out at Pentecost to drive the proclamation of the greater liberation.
